crafts.name: Tapis de Kairouan
crafts.history: The Kairouan carpet is one of the oldest and most prestigious Tunisian crafts. Its tradition dates back to the 9th century, tied to the founding of the holy city of Kairouan. Recognized by UNESCO, it represents a symbol of Tunisian and Maghrebi cultural heritage.
crafts.fun_fact: A high-quality Kairouan carpet can contain up to 160,000 knots per square meter, all tied by hand.

crafts.name: Poterie de Nabeul
crafts.history: Nabeul pottery is a tradition over 2,000 years old on the shores of Cap Bon. Inheriting Phoenician, Roman, and Andalusian traditions, it is famous for its hand-painted geometric and floral motifs. In the 16th century, Andalusian artisans expelled from Spain enriched its techniques.
crafts.fun_fact: The distinctive blue of Nabeul comes from cobalt oxide, once imported by Andalusian merchants in the 16th century — identical to the pigment used in the famous Delft faience of Holland.
crafts.time_to_create: 3 to 7 days for a complex piece

crafts.name: Bijoux Berbères
crafts.history: Tunisian Berber jewelry carries identity and spiritual protection. Made mainly by silversmiths on the island of Djerba and southern regions, it perpetuates a millennial tradition blending Berber, Phoenician, and Islamic influences. Each symbol has a precise meaning.
crafts.fun_fact: The Khamsa (five-fingered hand) is the most universal protective symbol in the Mediterranean — present in Jewish, Christian, and Muslim traditions since Antiquity.
crafts.time_to_create: 2 to 4 weeks for a complete necklace

crafts.name: Broderie Tunisienne
crafts.history: Tunisian embroidery is a refined textile art that varies by region. Monastir, Mahdia, and Hammamet each have their own signature motifs and colors. It adorns caftans, tablecloths, cushions, and wedding costumes. During the Ottoman period, Turkish embroidery influences enriched local techniques.
crafts.fun_fact: Monastir embroidery exclusively uses real gold thread for ceremonial garments — a tradition maintained since the Ottoman era.
crafts.time_to_create: 3 to 6 months for a complete dress

crafts.name: Djebba
crafts.history: The djebba is the quintessential Tunisian traditional garment, worn by men for centuries. A long tunic with wide sleeves, it is made in the medinas of Tunis, Sfax, and Sousse. It can be simple cotton for everyday wear or richly embroidered for ceremonies. The wedding djebba, adorned with golden motifs, is a true artisanal masterpiece.
crafts.fun_fact: Making a wedding djebba can take several artisans 3 to 4 months. Every embroidered stitch follows motifs passed down orally through generations.

crafts.name: Maroquinerie Tunisienne
crafts.history: The souks of the Medina of Tunis have housed tanners and leather craftsmen since the 11th century. The UNESCO-listed Medina of Tunis still houses tanning pits over 800 years old. Tunisian leather craft produces babouches, bags, belts, and book bindings.
crafts.fun_fact: The tanning pits of the Medina of Tunis are still in use today, using methods unchanged for over 800 years — lime, pigeon droppings, and pomegranate bark.

crafts.name: Bois Sculpté
crafts.history: Wood carving is a millennial tradition in the regions of Tunis, Sfax, and the forested areas of the north. Artisans transform thuya, olive, and cedar roots and branches into art objects. Marquetry — inlaying wood of different colors — reaches an exceptional level of refinement in the Medina.
crafts.fun_fact: Thuya wood from Kroumirie is considered the most precious wood in North Africa. Pliny the Elder, a Roman historian of the 1st century, already described it as the most expensive wood in the known world.
crafts.time_to_create: 1 week (simple box) to several months (marquetry furniture)

crafts.name: Ferronnerie Tunisienne
crafts.history: Tunisian ironworkers have adorned the doors, windows, lanterns, and grilles of traditional houses for centuries. Their art blends Islamic geometry with the Andalusian heritage of the 15th century. Ironwork from Kairouan and Tunis is renowned for its arabesque motifs and geometric stars. Some forged doors of the Medina of Tunis are over 400 years old.
crafts.fun_fact: The forged doors of the Medina of Tunis are classified as national heritage. Some, over 400 years old, still function perfectly — proof of the exceptional durability of hand-forged iron.

crafts.name: Dinanderie
crafts.history: Dinanderie — the art of working copper and brass — is one of the oldest traditions of the Medina of Tunis. In the Souk of the Coppersmiths (Souk el Nahhasin), artisans have hammered trays, teapots, lamps, and vases for centuries. This tradition dates back to the Aghlabid era (9th century) and reached its peak under the Hafsids.
crafts.fun_fact: A large engraved copper tray from the Medina may have required over 100,000 hammer strikes. The sound of hammering in the Souk el Nahhasin has echoed through the Medina for over 1,000 years.
crafts.time_to_create: 3 days (small piece) to 3 weeks (large engraved tray)

crafts.name: Verre Soufflé
crafts.history: The art of blown glass in Tunisia continues a tradition dating back to Roman times. Workshops in Beja and the Medina of Tunis create pieces in vibrant, irreplaceable colors. This art nearly disappeared in the 20th century before being saved by master glassblowers passionate about preserving this fragile heritage.
crafts.fun_fact: Every piece of blown glass is physically unique — it is impossible to create two identical pieces. The master glassblower can never exactly reproduce the same piece twice.
